capacity.title: How Capacity Works
capacity.intro: Storage capacity follows a waterfall: raw physical capacity is reduced by several layers of overhead before you get usable space. Understanding each layer helps you size storage accurately.

waterfall.title: The Capacity Waterfall
waterfall.step1: Raw Capacity: Physical drive size × drive count × server count
waterfall.step2: Subtract: Parity/redundancy overhead (depends on RAID level)
waterfall.step4: Subtract: Filesystem overhead (ZFS slop, WAFL overhead, etc.)
waterfall.step5: Subtract: System reserves (Ceph nearfull, S2D rebuild reserve, etc.)
waterfall.step8: = Effective Capacity (what you can logically store)

capacity.keyPoint: Compression and deduplication are multiplicative, not additive. 2x compression + 2x dedup = 4x effective capacity, not 3x. Overhead layers stack multiplicatively too: 10% parity + 5% filesystem ≠ 15% total loss.

raid.title: RAID & Redundancy Explained
raid.intro: RAID levels balance three competing goals: capacity efficiency, performance, and fault tolerance. No single RAID level wins on all three.

efficiency.title: Efficiency Formulas
efficiency.raid0: RAID 0: 100% efficiency — no redundancy, no fault tolerance
efficiency.raid1: RAID 1 / RAID 10: 50% efficiency — mirror, survives 1 drive failure per pair
efficiency.raid5: RAID 5: (n−1)/n efficiency — single parity, survives 1 drive failure
efficiency.raid6: RAID 6: (n−2)/n efficiency — double parity, survives 2 drive failures

writePenalty.title: Write Penalty
writePenalty.intro: Parity-based RAID levels have a write amplification penalty for random writes:
writePenalty.raid5: RAID 5: 4x penalty — read old data + read old parity + write new data + write new parity
writePenalty.raid6: RAID 6: 6x penalty — same as RAID-5 but with two parity calculations
writePenalty.mirror: RAID 1/10: 2x penalty — write to both mirror halves
writePenalty.sequential: Sequential writes have lower penalty (~1.5x for RAID-5) because full-stripe writes avoid the read-modify-write cycle.
writePenalty.sources: Sources: WintelGuy.com RAID Performance Calculator, Huawei Storage Knowledge Base

performance.title: Performance Bottlenecks
performance.intro: Storage performance is limited by the weakest link in a chain of four layers. Upgrading a non-bottleneck layer has zero effect on overall performance.

chain.title: The Bottleneck Chain
chain.media: Media Layer: Aggregate of all drive IOPS/throughput (sum of individual drives, adjusted for RAID write penalty)
chain.controller: Controller/HBA Layer: Fixed ceiling of the RAID controller or HBA (e.g., Broadcom 9500: 4M IOPS, 28 GB/s)
chain.pcie: PCIe Bus Layer: Generation × lanes (e.g., Gen4 x8 = ~16 GB/s, Gen5 x16 = ~64 GB/s)
chain.network: Network Layer: Link speed (e.g., 10 GbE = 1.25 GB/s, 25 GbE = 3.1 GB/s, 100 GbE = 12.5 GB/s)

performance.keyPoint: The layer with the lowest throughput is the bottleneck (highlighted in orange in the dashboard). Always upgrade the bottleneck first.
performance.alignment: For XFS filesystems on RAID arrays, stripe alignment (sunit/swidth) should match the RAID chunk size to avoid read-modify-write penalties on sequential I/O.

resilience.title: Resilience & Monte Carlo Simulation
resilience.intro: The resilience engine estimates the probability of surviving one year without data loss using Monte Carlo simulation — a statistical technique that runs thousands of randomized scenarios.

process.title: How the Simulation Works
process.step1: 1. Simulate random drive failures based on Annual Failure Rate (AFR)
process.step2: 2. Calculate rebuild time based on drive capacity and rebuild speed
process.step3: 3. During rebuild, calculate probability of Unrecoverable Read Error (URE)
process.step4: 4. Simulate correlated failures (10% chance a failure triggers another within 7 days)
process.step5: 5. Account for stress-induced failures during rebuild (1.3x higher failure rate)

ure.title: URE Risk During Rebuild
ure.description: A URE occurs when a bit on a surviving drive cannot be read during rebuild. The probability depends on the drive's URE rate (10⁻¹⁴ to 10⁻¹⁷) and the amount of data read. Larger drives have exponentially higher URE risk during rebuild — this is why RAID-5 with large (10TB+) drives is risky.

resilience.keyPoint: Small differences in survival rate represent large differences in absolute risk: 99.9% = 1 in 1,000 arrays lose data; 99.99% = 1 in 10,000; 99.999% = 1 in 100,000.
resilience.sources: Methodology based on industry-standard Markov chain failure analysis and AFR-based reliability modeling.

sustainability.title: Sustainability & TCO
sustainability.intro: Total Cost of Ownership extends beyond the hardware purchase to include energy, cooling, and environmental impact over the project lifetime.

pue.title: Power Usage Effectiveness (PUE)
pue.description: PUE is the ratio of total facility power to IT equipment power. A PUE of 1.4 means 40% of power goes to cooling and infrastructure.
pue.formula: Total Power = (Drive Power + Server Power) × PUE
pue.examples: Typical values: Best hyperscale ~1.06, Good enterprise ~1.3, Average ~1.55, Older facilities ~2.0+

co2.title: Carbon Intensity by Region
co2.description: CO₂ emissions depend on the local electricity grid. The same hardware in different locations produces vastly different emissions:
co2.comparison: Switzerland: 30 gCO₂/kWh (hydro) vs. Germany: 385 gCO₂/kWh (coal/gas mix) — a 12x difference for identical hardware.

endurance.title: SSD Endurance (DWPD)
endurance.description: DWPD (Drive Writes Per Day) measures how many times the full drive capacity can be written per day over its warranty period. Exceeding the rated DWPD accelerates wear and risks premature failure.
endurance.formula: Required DWPD = Daily Write Volume / Drive Capacity
endurance.example: Example: Writing 2 TB/day to a 1 TB SSD requires 2 DWPD. If the drive is rated for 1 DWPD, it will wear out at 2x the expected rate.

platforms.title: Platform-Specific Notes
platforms.intro: Each storage platform has unique behaviors that affect capacity and performance calculations. These are the most important platform-specific considerations.

zfs.title: ZFS
zfs.slop: Slop Space: ZFS reserves space to prevent the pool from reaching 100% full. Formula: MAX(128 MiB, MIN(capacity/32, 128 GiB)). For pools under ~4 TiB, this is ~3.1% of capacity. For larger pools, it caps at 128 GiB.
zfs.ashift: Ashift: Must match the drive's physical sector size. Use ashift=12 (4K) for modern drives. Mismatched ashift causes write amplification and performance degradation.

vsanEsa.title: VMware vSAN ESA
vsanEsa.architecture: Single-tier NVMe: ESA uses one all-NVMe tier — no cache/capacity disk groups. Drives attach directly to PCIe, so there is no RAID controller in the I/O path (Raidy drops the controller layer from the ESA bottleneck chain).
vsanEsa.raid1: RAID-1 (Mirror): FTT=1 stores 2 copies for 50% efficiency and needs at least 3 hosts (2n+1 hosts for FTT=n). Raidy models ESA RAID-1 for 2-node clusters; 3+ node clusters should prefer erasure coding.
vsanEsa.adaptive: Adaptive RAID-5: vSAN picks the stripe width by host count — 2+1 (67% efficiency) on 3–5 hosts, 4+1 (80% efficiency) on 6+ hosts — and resizes automatically as hosts are added or removed.
vsanEsa.raid6: RAID-6 (4+2): tolerates two failures at 67% efficiency, minimum 6 hosts.
vsanEsa.performance: RAID-5/6 at RAID-1 speed: a log-structured design coalesces incoming writes into full stripes, so erasure-coded writes avoid the read-modify-write penalty of the older OSA (which paid roughly 4×/6× on RAID-5/6).
vsanEsa.compression: Compression is per storage policy and applied before data crosses the network (up to ~4:1 on compressible data). Cluster-wide deduplication is not part of base ESA — global dedup arrives with VMware Cloud Foundation 9.
vsanEsa.reserve: Reserved capacity: keep roughly 25–30% free for operations reserve plus host-rebuild reserve. Auto-Policy Management can set a cluster-optimized default policy. vSAN rebuilds from this distributed slack space — there are no dedicated hot spares.
vsanEsa.source: Source: VMware/Broadcom TechDocs — vSAN ESA 'RAID-5/6 with the performance of RAID-1', adaptive RAID-5 erasure coding, reserved-capacity concepts

ceph.title: Ceph
ceph.nearfull: Nearfull Threshold: Ceph defaults to 85% nearfull ratio. At this point, OSDs begin warning. At 90% (backfillfull), recovery is denied. At 95% (full), writes are blocked entirely. Raidy calculates usable capacity at the 85% threshold.
ceph.ec: Erasure Coding: Common profiles include 2+1 (67%), 4+2 (67%), 8+3 (73%), and 8+4 (67%). Higher data-to-parity ratios improve efficiency but require more nodes.
ceph.source: Source: Ceph documentation (mon_osd_nearfull_ratio), Red Hat Ceph Storage documentation

s2d.title: Storage Spaces Direct (S2D)
s2d.resiliency: Resiliency & efficiency: two-way mirror (50%, min 2 nodes), three-way mirror (33%, min 3 nodes — recommended for production), single parity ((N−1)/N, min 3 nodes, supported but not recommended for clustered S2D), dual parity ((N−2)/N: 50% at 4 nodes rising to 87.5% at 16, min 4 nodes), and mirror-accelerated parity (mirror + parity blend, min 4 nodes).
s2d.replication: Replication & fault domains: every node is a fault domain. A mirror keeps one full copy per node and tolerates simultaneous node and drive failures; dual parity tolerates two failures. Clusters span 2–16 nodes.
s2d.reserve: Rebuild Reserve: leave one capacity drive per server free for in-place repair, capped at 4 drives cluster-wide — not a whole node. This lets S2D rebuild after a drive failure without replacing hardware.
s2d.nested: 2-node clusters: use nested resiliency (nested two-way mirror ≈25%, or nested mirror-accelerated parity ≈35–40%) instead of plain two-way mirror, so the cluster survives a drive failure during a node outage.
s2d.map: MAP (Mirror-Accelerated Parity): combines a ~20% mirror tier for hot data with a ~80% dual-parity tier for cold data, balancing performance and capacity efficiency.
s2d.refs: ReFS/CSV: volumes are ReFS on Cluster Shared Volumes. Metadata overhead is small (~2%) and is protected by the same resiliency as the data.
s2d.source: Source: Microsoft Learn — Storage Spaces Direct fault tolerance & nested resiliency

nutanix.title: Nutanix
nutanix.ec: EC-X: Applies to cold data only. RF2 + EC (4/1) achieves ~80% efficiency. RF3 + EC (4/2) achieves ~67%. Hot data remains replicated at the base RF level for write performance.
nutanix.overhead: System Overhead: Nutanix reserves ~10% of raw capacity for snapshots, metadata, CVM, and rebuild operations.
